cases.name: every canonical type in one row
cases.note: The whole contract in a single row: an int id, an exact decimal string, a 64-bit integer past 2^53, an ISO-8601 timestamp with no zone, and text.

sql.mssql: SELECT id, total, big_ref, placed_at, note FROM orders WHERE id = @p0
sql.mysql: SELECT id, total, big_ref, placed_at, note FROM orders WHERE id = ?
sql.postgres: SELECT id, total, big_ref, placed_at, note FROM orders WHERE id = $1
sql.sqlite: SELECT id, total, big_ref, placed_at, note FROM orders WHERE id = ?

cases.name: a NULL is a present column holding null
cases.note: Absent and null are different answers. A driver that drops the key reports 'no such column' where the database said 'no value'.

sql.mssql: SELECT big_ref, note FROM orders WHERE id = @p0
sql.mysql: SELECT big_ref, note FROM orders WHERE id = ?
sql.postgres: SELECT big_ref, note FROM orders WHERE id = $1
sql.sqlite: SELECT big_ref, note FROM orders WHERE id = ?

cases.name: a BIGINT past 2^53 is exact
cases.note: 9007199254740993 is 2^53 + 1. Any port that routes a 64-bit integer through a double returns ...992 instead, silently. This is also why the corpus encodes ints as digit strings.

sql.mssql: SELECT big_ref FROM orders WHERE id = @p0
sql.mysql: SELECT big_ref FROM orders WHERE id = ?
sql.postgres: SELECT big_ref FROM orders WHERE id = $1
sql.sqlite: SELECT big_ref FROM orders WHERE id = ?

cases.name: a DECIMAL keeps every digit
cases.note: 1234567.89 is the value MSSQL used to return as a lossy float. SQLite reaches the exact string only because the seed stores decimals as TEXT — a NUMERIC column there has REAL affinity and loses it at rest.

sql.mssql: SELECT total FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.mysql: SELECT total FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.postgres: SELECT total FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.sqlite: SELECT total FROM orders ORDER BY id

cases.name: a timestamp is ISO-8601 with no invented timezone
cases.note: These columns carry no timezone. The Postgres driver flags its DateTime UTC, so an unnormalized port appends a Z that asserts something the column never stored.

sql.mssql: SELECT placed_at FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.mysql: SELECT placed_at FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.postgres: SELECT placed_at FROM orders ORDER BY id
sql.sqlite: SELECT placed_at FROM orders ORDER BY id

cases.name: a BOOLEAN column, where the engines honestly differ
cases.note: Only Postgres (and MSSQL's BIT) have a real boolean type. MySQL's BOOLEAN is pure syntactic sugar for TINYINT(1) — the server reports a BOOLEAN column and a TINYINT(1) column IDENTICALLY, so a driver that maps width-1 tinyints to bool turns a stored 5 into true (verified). SQLite has no boolean at all. Faking one on either would be a lie, and levelling Postgres down to 1/0 would degrade a richer engine to match the weakest, so the divergence is recorded.

sql.mssql: SELECT id, is_active FROM customers ORDER BY id
sql.mysql: SELECT id, is_active FROM customers ORDER BY id
sql.postgres: SELECT id, is_active FROM customers ORDER BY id
sql.sqlite: SELECT id, is_active FROM customers ORDER BY id

cases.name: a join returns both sides in column order
cases.note: Column ORDER is part of the contract, not incidental — a consumer reading positionally must see the same shape everywhere.

sql.mssql: SELECT c.email, o.total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id ORDER BY o.id
sql.mysql: SELECT c.email, o.total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id ORDER BY o.id
sql.postgres: SELECT c.email, o.total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id ORDER BY o.id
sql.sqlite: SELECT c.email, o.total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id ORDER BY o.id

cases.name: an aggregate count is an integer
cases.note: COUNT(*) is a 64-bit integer on every engine here; it must not arrive as a decimal string or a float.

sql.mssql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM orders
sql.mysql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM orders
sql.postgres: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM orders
sql.sqlite: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM orders

cases.name: a zone-carrying column keeps its offset, normalized to UTC
cases.note: The bug this case exists to prevent (found 2026-07-21): normalization used to flatten EVERY Date into the zone-less form, so TIMESTAMPTZ '10:00+00' and '10:00-04' became two DIFFERENT naive strings and which ones you got depended on the reading machine's TZ. An instant identifies a point in time, so it must keep a designator; UTC specifically, because the driver hands over a Date that no longer remembers the offset it was written with. Both literals below name the SAME instant as the other row's, 4 hours apart in wall-clock terms.

sql.mssql: SELECT CAST('2024-04-01T10:00:00+00:00' AS DATETIMEOFFSET) AS a, CAST('2024-04-01T10:00:00-04:00' AS DATETIMEOFFSET) AS b
sql.postgres: SELECT TIMESTAMPTZ '2024-04-01 10:00:00+00' AS a, TIMESTAMPTZ '2024-04-01 10:00:00-04' AS b

cases.name: a DATE is a date, with no invented midnight
cases.note: A date column has no time component, so appending T00:00:00 states a midnight it never held — and once written it is indistinguishable from a timestamp that genuinely landed on midnight. This also guards the off-by-a-DAY case: tedious reads a zone-less value as UTC where pg and mysql2 read it as local, so taking the wrong half of the Date moved this date to 2024-03-31 west of Greenwich. SQLite is excluded because it has no date type at all — the seed stores ISO text, which needs no normalization and would not exercise this.

sql.mssql: SELECT CAST('2024-04-01' AS DATE) AS d
sql.mysql: SELECT CAST('2024-04-01' AS DATE) AS d

cases.name: a zone-less timestamp reads the same in every timezone
cases.note: Case 4 pins the FORM; this pins the INDEPENDENCE. Run the suite under TZ=America/New_York, TZ=Asia/Tokyo and TZ=UTC and this golden must not move. It caught tedious reading DATETIME2 as UTC while pg and mysql2 read as local, which shifted MSSQL by the reader's offset — green on a UTC CI box, wrong on a developer's laptop.

sql.mssql: SELECT CAST('2024-04-01T10:00:00' AS DATETIME2) AS t
sql.mysql: SELECT CAST('2024-04-01 10:00:00' AS DATETIME) AS t

cases.name: an array of temporals is normalized element-wise
cases.note: Postgres is the only dialect here with an array type, so this case is Postgres-only — the honest surface, not a portability gap. It exists because leaving containers alone was NOT neutral: with no kind registered for the array OIDs the elements passed through as raw driver objects, so this same row read as ["2024-04-01T14:00:00.000Z"] in America/New_York and ["2024-04-01T01:00:00.000Z"] in Asia/Tokyo, and the date[] landed on 2024-03-31. Each element takes the form its own type already has: naive keeps no designator, date keeps no time, and the instant keeps its Z. The text[] proves a non-temporal array is left alone.

sql.postgres: SELECT ARRAY[TIMESTAMP '2024-04-01 10:00:00', TIMESTAMP '2024-04-02 11:30:00'] AS ts_arr, ARRAY[DATE '2024-04-01'] AS date_arr, ARRAY[TIMESTAMPTZ '2024-04-01 10:00:00+00'] AS tstz_arr, ARRAY['a','b'] AS txt_arr

cases.name: a multi-dimensional array recurses
cases.note: Split out from the flat-array case so ONE port being blocked cannot cost the other its coverage. `package:postgres` 3.5.12 mis-decodes every multi-dimensional array — not just temporal ones: ARRAY[ARRAY[1,2],ARRAY[3,4]] comes back as [1, 1]. Its readListBytes consumes exactly ONE (dim_size, lower_bound) pair, but the wire format carries one PER DIMENSION, so at ndim=2 it starts reading elements from inside the second dimension's header. Casting the same value ::text returns {{"2024-04-01 10:00:00"}}, which proves the server sent the right bytes and the decoder loses them. The golden stays correct and the Dart port records the gap explicitly rather than the contract being weakened to match a driver bug.
